transcript: Welcome to Behind the Ticker, the podcast where we go beyond the symbol and into the strategy. I'm Brad Roth, founder and chief investment officer at Thor Funds. And in each episode, I sit down with ETF managers, CIOS, and industry leaders to break down how these funds are actually built, how they behave in real markets, and how advisers use them in real portfolios. Most people just see a ticker symbol, but we know much more goes on behind the ticker. [music] >> Hey David, welcome to the show. >> I mean, great to be here. Excited to be on the show. >> So, before we get started, why don't you take just a little bit of your time, tell everybody about uh a little bit about your background. If I did my research correctly, looks like you were a financial adviser for over 20 years and now you're building out this suite of ETFs under X Funds. So, how did you go from running a wealth management practice to now launching ETFs? >> Yeah, you know, it's a pretty interesting transition. Had a great career. I was 20 years financial adviser. We still have a private wealth firm that we run here in Atlanta, but yeah, it really started during COVID. We we my background we did a lot of insurancebased strategies where if you look at the average insurance company's balance sheet uh they are 90 95% fixed income and then they take a little bit of the interest on the fixed income and they typically go long long call options to get some upside exposure. So, you know, with FIX, which was our first fund, we thought, you know, what if we could recreate what the insurance companies are doing, but do it in a liquid rapper. And so, if you know of fixed annuities, you know, these are generally longer term contracts, 5, 8, 10year contracts. So, the whole idea was like, hey, can we take that and and and make it liquid? And so, we had clients asking for treasuries. We didn't really want to charge fees. So we came up with a way that we could create a product where we could hopefully get a little bit of a premium to treasuries and keep it in a liquid rapper. So that was our first fund. Uh you know it it saw some success and it's been great. We had some big institutional investors in that a few uh foreign governments which used some of their treasury allocation to to invest in FIX. So it's been great. So that was our first fund and then GIX was our second. Uh, Blocks was our third crypto fund and then we have four more I would say along the lines of Blocks, four more thematic funds, our silver, gold, defense and nuclear fund. So really excited and we have two additional funds coming out uh here shortly that are crypto that are Bitcoin based funds or overnight fund and a hedge uh Bitcoin tail hedge fund. So lots of stuff and I think we got a few more that's in the lab right now that we're working on. So this is this is really exciting for us and it's a big year of growth going from three funds to uh you know within a month we'll have gosh nine funds. So so pretty exciting. >> No that is exciting and I I definitely want to get into the business and some of your uh products today specifically. But before we get you know down to business, what do you like to do for fun? Any hobbies when you're not behind the desk or thinking about, you know, the next new fund you're going to launch? Yeah, you know, I'm I I love being with my kids and my family. So, it's for me being a dad is just the best. Probably sounds cliche, but you know, I grew up single mom, didn't have a dad around, and so I've always wanted to be just the best dad I could be. So, it's it's again, it's balancing. There's a lot of work to do, and we work really hard here. But if I'm not here, I'm at home with my kids. So, yeah, I mean, we love to travel. We love to uh my son loves cars, fast cars. So, you know, we love, you know, going and looking at cars and racing cars and uh traveling together. I mean, really, it's if I'm not here, I'm with family. And, you know, I try to read when I can, but it's usually early mornings. But family travels pretty much sums it up. >> Yeah. It's funny. Our our sons are similar. Uh he likes fast cars, too. He always he sits in the back of uh sits in the back of my car, goes, "Go fast, daddy." And I don't know where he got the southern draw, but anytime he's in the back, man, he just wants to go fast. >> I love it. I love it. I know. It's I I get all three. I have two girls and a and a boy and I can fit them all in my car and we just we fly and so they they love it. >> Yeah. Yeah. >> So, let's talk about X funds. As you as you noted, you started with FI AX back in 2022. Uh and then you went to GX and then blocks um which is BL X which has just crossed 100 million in assets. And so now you've just launched four new funds as you said. We've got silver, gold, nuclear, and weapon at a very high level. What's the common thread across all of these funds? >> Yeah. So, so blocks actually we we are a little over 200 just under 250 million, which has been unbelievable. I I I would say the common kind of thread between them all is really it's this idea that you have a core asset and then you've got an options overlay that can generate additional income. And so the with the blocks and the fourth thematic funds, what we really I think we're the first to really do this is it's half commodity and then half companies that benefit from that commodity doing well. And so with blocks, obviously it's it's 40 to 50% Bitcoin and Ethereum and then you've got the companies that benefit from the the blockchain or crypto. For our silver fund, we're we have silver as the commodity as as the sleeve, but then we have the silver miners. So, silver royalty companies, silver construction companies. Same thing with gold, right? Gold as a as a commodity. You have the ecosystem of gold. Uh weapon is a little unique in that the commodity are are really is the rare earths. So, it's all the rare earth materials that go into a lot of our defense sector that we need for ships and planes and ammunition and missiles. And then we balance that with our traditional uh defense companies. And then the same thing for our nuclear ETF as well. You get the commodity and then you actually have the the nuclear industry company. So really unique there. There really wasn't a lot of funds like that really any funds that balance the both together but then add the options overlay. And we want our options overlay to be uh additive not detract. So and it's the way we structure our options. we use spreads generally versus just straight covered calls or uh short puts. So, it's really unique in that structure for the funds. >> Yeah, we're going to get we're going to get into that option overlay here in a second, but more of a business or philosophical question. I mean, you've launching you launching four new funds and you know, you just mentioned to me you're launching more pretty much simultaneously, right? Walk me through the decision to come out with four all at once. I mean it's pretty ambitious especially those of us who you know are are on the smaller side of the issuance uh smaller side of issuers. So can you just talk us through the business decision about hey look we're really passionate about this we've seen some success these are the right ones let's do it now. Yeah, it's actually a really uh important question because we really launched one fund a year for the last three years. And so if you think about that, you know, yeah, there's a lot of work that goes into launching a fund. And you know, again, we don't have economies of scale like a Vanguard or a Black Rock or a State Street uh that can list and launch funds no issues whatsoever. So it it takes a lot of of carry to get these funds off the ground from a compliance perspective, marketing standpoint, expense. So right, I I would tell you that it's actually because of our investors that have believed in us and invested in the funds. FIX once we crossed over 100 million, we got ready to start our second fund. GIX, we saw some uh early success on that as well. So we started to launch our third fund. Blocks immediately shot up to 100 million almost overnight. So then we started moving the uh the needle on our other funds. And so I think what we saw over the last three years is that if you put out a product that is unique and that has good performance, the AUM will follow. And so instead of delaying these out, we just said, you know what, and that this is we're taking, you know, I'm publicly sharing this, but I'm basically taking all of our revenue that we were getting off these funds, a majority of it, and pouring it back into these new six funds that are being launched. So again, it's me with a lot of faith saying we're going to bet everything on these funds as we go forward. Not in a risky way, just in, hey, we're going to take what our our investors have invested in us so far. Use that revenue to go give them more value from a fund standpoint and hopefully it'll be a success and and it'll build on itself. >> Yeah. No, it's great. It's it's conviction in yourself and your team and and your, you know, your product and, you know, delayed gratification, I guess. uh because you know the idea there would be hopefully revenues are a lot bigger with more funds and >> so let's talk about the option overlay you just you just touched on it a bit it's really the engine across your entire suite right for somebody who might not be familiar can you walk everybody through how the define risk option strategy actually works and how it how it generates income >> yeah so it's it's really interesting in that we've got uh FIX is a little different I would say than than the uh other six seven funds in that it's we really use some unique structures in that where we're doing a um a really risk defined trade. So in in FIX just quickly to and I'll address the other ones but in FIX is really new we're actually using a high we have a treasury underlay but we're also we're doing short spreads on HYG which is a junk bond ETF. So, we're generating the yield. We're we're we're harvesting the yield from our HYG spread. And then all of the yield that we capture from that spread, we go and buy uh long index call options. And so really what we're doing is if if the S&P is negative over the over the term of our expiration, all we really risked was the income that we generated from our HY spread. And so it's actually a way to get a lowrisk exposure to the market. So we're actually paying for our our long call exposure. So that's one element of it. And then we also do some either short calls or short put spreads, what we call opportunistic trades. So we may have monthlies on where we are, I think we are long XLU right now, long XLF, short XLP. So it's like you've got the long index exposure going on in the background, but then you have these monthly kind of opportunistic trades that go on as well. So that keeping FIX separate. Everything else is really unique in that we generally are doing a structure where we're selling put spreads on our equity positions that we have high conviction in. And one of my challenges always been with cover call ETFs is cover call ETFs have rarely ever beat the underlying. So if the market rips higher and you have a short call on, right, you're limiting your upside gain. Excuse me. So what we did is on the short put spreads is we can capture all of the upside without really capping our upside and then on the ETF side is where we're selling our call spreads. So lower volatile assets but the ability to earn some higher income as well. So, you said something super interesting there, which is I believe it's in uh is the fixed income and annuity product about how you're actually long some asset classes, you're short some asset classes, like is that an active ongoing decision or is it just kind of part of uh the portfolio construction process? And if it is an active decision, like what is driving that decision? What's the process there? >> Yeah. So, with FIX is really unique. So we you'll see like early on we were doing a lot of opportunistic trades. Every month we were doing an opportunistic trade and we have a lot of algos that we run but what we found out was short-term opportunistic trades we really were lagging the it was just tough to beat the indexes right in a year like 2023 or 24 20 markets were just shooting straight higher. And so what we decided to do is actually shift the strategy more recently is where we kind of have these 3 to six month long call exposure on. So we let the market do its thing and then we're going to get broad exposure through all the ups and downs because what we found out was in April of 25 when we had the tariff issues we had some options that were expiring right then right end of March end of April and it was just bad timing. And so to avoid that, we do these longerdated 3 to six month long call exposure to the indexes. And then right, we have these opportunistic trades where we have a we have an algo that we run, but we also I'll give you some of the simple stuff that everyone knows about, right? Like you can look at relative strength levels for a lot of these ETFs. And so like if you look at XLP right now, uh XLP has just been on a tear, consumer staples. And why? Well, people are concerned about the economy slowing down. you know, there's tech's been overvalued, so we've been buying staples. Well, if you look at from an RSI standpoint, it's completely overbought. And so, that would be one of the first areas that we look at. What's the strength? Uh, overbought, oversold indicators for a certain sector. Um, XLP is very overbought at this point. So, that would be an example of a name that we have a short position on currently. And then a name like XLF XLU are going to be names that if we have conviction on them, maybe they are oversold, so it makes sense to go and do a short-term trade on them. Or there's just a macro theme around interest rates uh or around growth that we think would benefit those sectors. So again, very opportunistic, but we're taking half a percent risk on each of those trades. So we have three of those trades on right now in FIX. They're all half a percent risk each. One and a half% max loss in all three of those. We don't want to lose on all three of those, but I can tell you XLP is probably going to be down when XLU and XLF are up. And so we can actually kind of ma manage those trades where if we see one that's up, we may take profits early. But again, treasuries are yielding four. All we need is 30 basis points per month of additional return over a 12-month period. That's three and a half%. So you're at 7 and a half, 8% of additional yield added over by the Treasury. So it doesn't take a lot. And we we usually can do that with those opportunistic trades. >> That makes a ton of sense. Thanks for going into that with me. But let's let's get back to let's get to the topic at hand, which is these new funds you just launched. I couldn't uh there couldn't be better timing to talk about your uh Nicholas Silver Income ETF, SLVX. Walk me through the structure. From what I understand, you've got an equity sleeve with miners, the commodity sleeve with actual exposure, and then the option overlay on top. So, how do these three pieces work together? >> Yeah, I I really like silver here. And, you know, I look at silver as like a dual demand metal. It's it's um it can act as a hedge, a monetary hedge, but it's also an industrial input. So, in that that's a little bit different from gold, whereas gold generally acts as your hedge against geopolitical or monetary risk where silver does have that industrial component. And so, it it participates in these precious metal cycles like we've seen in the last year, right? whether it's rates, dollar liquidity, uh geopolitical risk, but it really is a bet on where we think industrial growth is headed. And so I do think there's a lot of we've already seen it, but I I do think there's a use case for silver in the next 3 to 5 years that the market still is not fully pricing in, but the the way that we we're structuring the portfolio, uh I can just give you from a an allocation standpoint. So really what we have as a breakdown is I can give you some of the waitings here as this pulls up. So about 50% of the portfolio just roughly is going to be our silver equity companies. So these are going to be again when you think about I want to I want exposure to silver. I it's like we take the Warren Buffet approach. We want to own the whole silver ecos. So 50% are going to be silver miners uh or silver industry companies. So these are going to be names like first Majestic, Pan-American, Wheaton, uh Silver Corp, Fortuna. That's your equity sleeve. And then the other 50% is going to be actual silver spot exposure through silver ETFs. So like SLV, SIBR, uh PSLV. So these are going to be where we're getting that actual silver exposure marrying that with the equity positions and then we're doing our options overlay. So we we have we have a mandate where we can do call spreads, put spreads, but again if we are bullish on a sector like silver, I don't want to cap my upside on the silver equity positions because the silver equity positions can act as a leveraged trade to the price of silver. And so really what we're doing on that side is we are selling put spreads on the equity positions. So again, we sell a put spread at the money or a little bit below the money. Uh that position goes up in value. We capture all the premium from our put that we sold and we get the upside exposure in the stock. On the SLV positions, we start out selling call spreads. So that does cap part of our upside on the actual spot price of silver, but we're buying a long call on that uh underlying ETF as well. So, if it does rip, which we've seen silver rip, we're going to miss out on that little bit of a gap in the spread, but once it gets to our long strike, we're going to capture all the upside uh return from there. So, again, half of the portfolio is the equities, half the portfolio is the actual SLV exposure, silver exposure, and then we have the options overlay working on each side. So, uh we can hedge. So, just like blocks, we can buy protective puts or protective put spread. uh we can sell calls on the equity positions if we feel like there's some downward movement on it. So we have some broad mandates between the two of the two buckets. That's what I was going to say just listening to you uh talk you these these are less I would say traditional index and option overlay products because there is what it sounds like to me a pretty heavy active component underlying here where you guys are actually in the portfolio making some decisions um based on what's going on. It's not just a set it and forget it type product. Am I reading this right? >> Yeah. So we we don't track an index. So, a lot of ETFs, which are great, and I actually think there's a lot of value in tracking an index, especially a wellestablished index. Uh, none of our ETFs are index trackers, meaning they're more passive. So, they all are active funds. And so, when you're buying a a fund that's active, you're putting a lot of weight on the manager's ability to select positions and manage the fund. So, yeah, option strategy is completely actively managed. Um, we can put hedges on, take hedges off. We can switch from calls to puts at any moment. So it really is a lot of it's driven by you know our trade desk and and the management team. >> I love that. So you touched on the difference between you know silver and gold you know silver having the industrial use case and the extreme high demand for it now but you've also launched GLDN alongside uh which is your your gold cousin to silver. Um is there a difference in approach? Is it basically the same type of fund just with different exposures? So you're just looking at a different area of the market. >> That's exactly right. So it it's really just the exact same structure. So the build is exactly the same, right? Half gold companies uh and then half actual gold exposure and then we do the options overlay. So it really just comes down to what what is your base case on metals in that do you you know do you want the more defensive hedge against monetary policy which will be gold. uh if you continue to think that the price of gold will be supported and increase, which we do, that's going to positively impact miners. So if you look at the miners return over the price of gold, it's there's certainly been outperformance. So you're going to capture it there. So yeah, same structure, same fund, just silver versus gold and the different use cases for each of those different asset classes. >> Yeah, one of the criticisms that I've I've heard of option overlay strategies is that the income can mask what's really happening, the total return. um you know the investor sees a big distribution but the NAV you know might continue to decline. It sounds like to me I'm answering my own question is your ability to be active in some of these funds is how you're kind of managing that tradeoff. Would I be right to assume that? >> Yeah. So I would say that that is true for call spread products. Um, if you're like if a lot of the income is driven by the put spread strategy, which is which ours is, there's really, let's just say you had a fund that was only put spreads. So, you had the the underlying position and then the put spreads in a rising market. If those underlying positions were going higher, the option strategy wouldn't mask anything, right? Because the equities are positions are going up and you're not capping any of the upside, you're getting additional upside. So, I think where where there's a challenge is if you're in a rising market and you have a covered call income strategy. So, it's you're generating income, but you're not keeping up with the underlying uh positions that are in the portfolio where I think a strategy like ours is going to underperform. When you're selling put spreads, you're just going to have more volatility or you're going to have more downside exposure. So, you know, if if an asset class is down and you're selling put spreads, we're losing on our put spreads and we're losing on our equity positions as well. So, I think it's just understanding that. Uh, but again, if you look at or if you believe markets go up over time, which we know they do, and which I believe we may not go up every month or every year, but if you look at a 3, five, 10 year track record, equity markets head higher. So a put spread strategy generally is going to outperform a call strategy just because you're not capping that upside. But the NAV decline is to address really your question I think is the there's the distribution rate which a fund can declare any distribution rate they want right and so we could say hey we're going to declare a 50% distribution rate. It may just be that these underlying positions go up 50%. And we distributed the the the cap gains. We sold some of these positions. But I like to tie the distribution rate to exactly what the options yield is generating. So for example, in blocks, we're actually generating closer to 50% yield off of our options positions. We're distributing 30 36%. So we could declare tomorrow, and there's some other crypto competitor funds that are 40 50% yield. We could easily declare a 40 50% yield on blocks, but the idea is that you're probably going to see some significant NAD decline because of that distribution. With blocks, we distribute 36 because we know that gives us about an 80% success rate. If we're 80% successful on a 50% yield, that should be about a um 70 80% it's going to get us close to that 36 to 40% yield that we're returning. So I just think overall is if you look at it there's two types of NAB decline. There's NAB decline because you're distributing more than the fund is appreciating or there's NAB decline that the fund is going down just because the underlying positions are going down. Like take blocks right I don't have the exact numbers in front of me but since inception the the price of Bitcoin uh is down I wish I had the exact numbers in front of me but but the price of Bitcoin is down something like 20 to 30%. So the underlying position, a big core of it is down yet blocks is somewhat flat over that time period. So that would say, well, David, maybe we're down slightly. Why? Well, you know, is that NAV decline? I would say, well, no, we're outperforming the underlying and we're not distributing enough to where it's dragging the fund down. So long answer, probably a little winded, but the I I'm good with a NAV going down because the underlying positions are going down. I don't want a NAFTA to go down because the fund's overdistributing. >> Yeah, makes a ton of sense to me. So I I think you know again some advisors have I would say got gotten a slow start to using commodities inside of a portfolio. You have some advisors that just love it. And you have some advisors that say hey gold and silver is not really an investment. It's more of a um you know it's it's a commodity that we don't really put clients in. So I would say if you're open, if you're an adviser that's putting your clients in gold and silver, I still think Warren Buffett, like right, you ask Warren Buffett about gold, silver, Bitcoin, they they don't touch it. They they he hated it. Uh obviously the price has done well, so it it made for a good investment. But I think if you're telling a client and I'll use it for us as our private wealth form, we had clients asking for Bitcoin and we're like, well, we can go out and just buy Bitcoin through an ETF or if I'm an adviser, do I just want the commodity or again, do I want exposure to the whole sector? And so I would think of it as like don't just buy the commodity, buy the whole sector. And again, it's the picks and shovels approach, right? It's like I want to own railroads. Great. So railroad is your commodity. Well, what about the companies that provide the shovels and that provide clearing of the land and the company that provides the railroad tracks, right? So, I would look at it as like if I want to own railroads, yeah, I want to sort of the railroad, but I want to really really where you make your money is on the ancillary, the picks and shovels approach. So, I would say that this is the ultimate picks and shovels approach where we have the commodity, but then we have the picks and shovels, which really the picks and shovels actually could outperform the actual commodity over time. >> Yeah, I would agree with that. No, it's it's a great question and I and I had to do this transition in our private wealth business where you know when you start a business and it's as you know it's tough to start a business and and but so much relies on you and so it you know in our private wealth space I had to intentionally remove myself from a lot of areas so that we could grow right and I would say in the ETF space our first three funds they were I mean they had my fingerprints all over them from construction to design to marketing to, you know, strategy outlook. And while that's great and they've done well, for us to grow and scale, it's just it's it's replacing the things that I can do with people that are even smarter than me. And I think that's where we're at right now is like we we are bringing on and hiring people that just, you know, they really this is what they do. This is what their skills are at. This is where they excel and they can really take us to a whole another level. And so yeah, so I would think it's this transition of of where I have all the decision-m is really driven by me to where we can I can only my ceiling is so much and so as we grow the only way we can keep up with the growth and push the growth is is to bring other people on.
